prompt: Explain what a "closure" is in programming, in exactly one sentence. The sentence can be long.
response: A closure is an inner function that maintains a private link (in the form of a closure environment or environment record) to the outer scope's local variables and bindings, even after the outer function has returned, effectively remembering its "lexical" or "lexical-scoping" environment, allowing it to access and reference the outer scope's variables, as though the inner function had been defined in the outer scope, enabling the inner function to mimic "non-local" or "dynamic" variable access, all in the absence of traditional "global" or "lexical" scoping, all thanks to the function's inner workings as a first-class value.
